Introduction
 
Chemotherapy
 
resistance
 
in
 
high-grade
 
serous
 
ovarian
 
cancer
 
(HGSC)
 
is
 
strongly
 
influenced
 
by
 
the
 
tumor
 
microenvironment
 
(TME)
 
where
 
tumor-host
 
interactions
 
are
 
critical.
 
Conventional
 
2D
 
models
 
lack
 
this
 
complexity,
 
limiting
 
their
 
predictive
 
value.
 
To
 
address
 
this,
 
we
 
developed
 
a
 
patient-derived
 
3D
 
culture
 
model
 
incorporating
 
cancer,
 
immune
 
cells,
 
and
 
cancer-associated
 
fibroblasts
 
(CAFs)
 
embedded
 
in
 
human
 
omentum-derived
 
extracellular
 
matrix
 
(ECM),
 
capturing
 
the
 
TME
 
architecture
 
and
 
diversity.
 
In
 
this
 
proof-of-concept
 
study
 
we
 
applied
 
multiplexed
 
single-cell
 
RNA
 
sequencing
 
(scRNA-seq)
 
to
 
compare
 
drug-treated
 
cultures
 
with
 
and
 
without
 
CAFs
 
to
 
uncover
 
how
 
CAFs
 
shape
 
treatment
 
responses.
 
Methodology
 
Samples
 
were
 
collected
 
within
 
the
 
Oncosys-OVA
 
study
 
(NCT06117384),
 
and
 
processed
 
using
 
an
 
optimized
 
enzymatic
 
dissociation
 
protocol,
 
isolating
 
cancer,
 
immune
 
cells,
 
and
 
CAFs.
 
We
 
generated
 
two
 
patient-matched
 
cultures:
 
with

using
 
an
 
optimized
 
enzymatic
 
dissociation
 
protocol,
 
isolating
 
cancer,
 
immune
 
cells,
 
and
 
CAFs.
 
We
 
generated
 
two
 
patient-matched
 
cultures:
 
with
 
(CAF+)
 
and
 
without
 
CAFs
 
(CAF–).
 
Cells
 
were
 
embedded
 
in
 
human
 
omentum-derived
 
ECM
 
and
 
cultured
 
under
 
serum-free
 
conditions,
 
and
 
treated
 
for
 
48
 
hours
 
with
 
standard
 
chemotherapy
 
or
 
targeted
 
agents.
 
The
 
cells
 
were
 
barcoded,
 
pooled,
 
and
 
processed
 
for
 
multiplexed
 
scRNA-seq
 
using
 
the
 
10x
 
Genomics
 
Chromium
 
platform.
 
Initial
 
analysis
 
focused
 
on
 
cell-type
 
annotation,
 
differential
 
gene
 
expression,
 
and
 
inference
 
of
 
intercellular
 
communication
 
networks.
 
Results
 
and
 
Discussion
 
Using
 
flow
 
cytometry
 
and
 
immunofluorescence,
 
we
 
confirmed
 
that
 
both
 
CAF–
 
and
 
CAF+
 
3D
 
cultures
 
maintained
 
viability,
 
preserved
 
cellular
 
composition,
 
and
 
retained
 
functional
 
characteristics
 
of
 
the
 
TME
 
during
 
culture,
 
enabling
 
controlled
 
comparisons
 
of
 
drug
 
responses
 
with
 
or
 
without
 
CAF
 
influence.
 
Multiplexed
 
scRNA-seq
 
yielded
 
high-quality
 
transcriptomes
 
across
 
treatment
 
conditions
 
and
 
cell
 
types.
 
Preliminary
 
analysis
 
demonstrates
 
the
 
feasibility
 
of
 
applying
 
scRNA-seq
 
to
 
complex,
 
fully
 
humanized

across
 
treatment
 
conditions
 
and
 
cell
 
types.
 
Preliminary
 
analysis
 
demonstrates
 
the
 
feasibility
 
of
 
applying
 
scRNA-seq
 
to
 
complex,
 
fully
 
humanized
 
patient-derived
 
3D
 
cultures,
 
providing
 
a
 
window
 
for
 
uncovering
 
cell-type-specific
 
drug
 
response
 
programs
 
influenced
 
by
 
CAFs.

Conclusion
 
We
 
present
 
a
 
proof-of-concept
 
application
 
of
 
single-cell
 
transcriptomics
 
to
 
a
 
physiologically
 
relevant
 
3D
 
HGSC
 
model.
 
Our
 
approach
 
enables
 
detailed
 
mapping
 
of
 
drug
 
responses
 
across
 
the
 
HGSC
 
tumor
 
ecosystem,
 
offering
 
a
 
promising
 
tool
 
for
 
preclinical
 
testing
 
and
 
biomarker
 
discovery.
